section_title: INTRODUCTION TO PART 4: IF I COULD GO BACK & TELL MYSELF WHAT I KNOW NOW
content:
This is part four of the 'If I Could Go Back & Tell Myself What I Know Now' series. This is a hypothetical
conversation with my younger self.

section_title: WHEN TO TRANSITION FROM DEMO TO LIVE TRADING
content:
When am I ready for live funds? It begins with proof of concept.
- You had to show minimum of six months consistency
- Managed to keep risk below three percent maximum
- Unemotionally weathered strings of losses
First and foremost, you have to have proof of concept.

section_title: AVOID INFORMATION OVERLOAD
content:
You're going to be doing a lot of things, buying a lot of books. I wish I could tell you to save all that money
because you're going to have over 2,000 books and less than a dozen of them were really worth the time
reading. A lot of it's going to be regurgitated things. You want to focus on the following things. If you do this,
Michael, you're going to be able to fast track your way to get through a lot of the hardships that you will
unknowingly enter yourself into and chase and pursue things that are going to lead to more loss and
confusion.

section_title: SEASONAL TENDENCIES
content:
The first thing you'll be focusing on is seasonal tendencies. You want to ignore the things that Jake
Bernstein talks about. The MRCI group that puts out seasonal tendencies, this person has the best
seasonal tendencies. Mrci.com is the website. You're going to find that if you stick to that gentleman's
seasonal tendencies, they're going to serve you exceptionally well.

section_title: QUARTERLY SHIFTS
content:
Quarterly shifts you'll be looking for moves that move every three to four months. Now the markets always
have some specific gyration, but focusing primarily on the major moves that should transpire over the next
three to four months. If you focus there, you're going to find a lot of these mega trades.

section_title: MONTHLY BIAS AND INTER-MARKET ALIGNMENT
content:
Monthly bias: try to formulate all of the things that you're doing every single day to get in sync with the
monthly bias. What is the monthly bias likely to do? Is it likely to trade higher? Is it likely to trade lower?
Focusing within that particular bias, within a specific quarterly shift, with any specific seasonal tendency is
going to give you the highest odds probability of success than anything you're going to read in any of the
books that you buy. So if I could grab you and shake you by the lapels, Michael, this is exactly what I would
stress to you because everything that you're doing prior to identifying this is creating the problem, all the
frustrations you're feeling, all that I want to know where it's going next. This is the three things that helps
figure that out for you. But it's going to be years before you discern that, but if I could convince you right
now, this is what I would tell you to do: focus on these top three things.

section_title: DEVELOPING YOUR WEEKLY PROFILE
content:
Once you have that determined, then you can focus on a weekly profile. Now if it's bullish, then you're
expecting to have a weekly profile that's bullish. At the time this recording, you're world renowned for
knowing and teaching how the weekly bias and weekly range is typically formed with the higher low formed
generally by Tuesday's London Open, if not by Wednesday. So if you can focus on that seasonal tendency
that's bullish or bearish, and you're looking for that quarterly shift that's in align with that seasonal
tendency, and you have factored into what the monthly bias should be in concert with those two previous
ideas, your weekly profile should be in alignment as well. So you're not trying to go against the underlying
momentum, you're trying to get in sync with the institutional order flow.

section_title: POWER OF 3 (AMD)
content:
Once you identify the seasonal tendency and you're focusing on a quarterly shift or a price swing that
should transpire over the next three to four months, and your monthly bias is determined to be bullish or
bearish relative to those seasonal tendencies in the next quarterly shift, and you're working with any
weekly profile that's bullish or bearish relative to the idea of that monthly bias and quarterly shift and
seasonal tenancy, the daily template, that means what type of day are we going to have? Did we just come
off of a big range day or a series of big range days because we're probably going to contract and go into
small ranges. But if we do go into a small range, it's going to be a short term event then it should lead to a
large expansion in the direction of the monthly bias in the expectation of the quarterly shift that you're
looking for and within alignment of the seasonal tendency. The daily template is basically your Power 3
concept where it's the open, high, low, and close formation of the daily bar. You're looking for accumulation,
manipulation, and distribution.

section_title: MASTERING TIME OF DAY
content:
You want to spend a lot of time studying time of day.
- London Open has specific characteristics that generally make the higher low of the day, not all the time
but most of the time.
- New York is a continuation of whatever has transpired in London, unless you've reached a higher time
frame level or key level that may introduce a New York session market reversal profile.
- London Close is typically the opposite end of the daily range or will be opposing the higher low formed in
London Open.
Focusing on a time of day within all these specific studies are going to put you in the highest order of
probability. Doesn't mean you're going to not take losses, you will. But sticking to this in this protocol is
going to lead to the longest stretch of consistency and continuity in your trading than you're ever going to
find doing anything else.

section_title: MINIMUM CONSISTENCY & THE PERILS OF RUSHING PROFITS
content:
Minimum six months consistency. Now I understand you're excited and you see there's a lot of things
ahead of you, Michael, and you've made that plan of retiring at 40 and news flash, you get there, but try not
to rush profits. There's going to be a transition you don't know when it's going to occur, but it is when you
start trading the bond market. The bond market starts serving you very well, and then you discover that you
can do far more per week than you could with just one lot by using more money management. And the
enticement is going to be: get there quicker, get there quicker. And you discover the power of compound
interest, but you don't at that time understand the depths that it can take you down with. It's very powerful,
but it's a double-edged sword.

section_title: MANAGING TRADE FREQUENCY AND AVOIDING INDICATOR TRAPS
content:
If I could change your mind about something, speaking to you now, you should do your best to try not to be
profitable or rush profits. And what do I mean by that? Taking more trades than necessary. In other words,
your trade frequency, Michael, when you first start is going to be horrendous. There's going to be days
where you actually have taken 30 entries and none of them are profitable because you're going back and
forth, back and forth, thinking you're going to get the right direction, and it's not going to work out for you.
This is the reason why you have to work with the previous information where you work from a macro
perspective down. If you do that, you will also be more controlled over your decision making and you won't
be in a rush to make profits. Rushing to profit is the surest, fastest way to lose money. It doesn't feel like it,
especially when you're buying all these books because all these books are sugar coating everything,
they're oversimplifying everything and giving you a false sense of security because of these indicators, and
the indicators are not the secret. Those indicators are just the red herring and the gimmick that that book's
using to sell.

section_title: FOCUS ON MONTHLY RETURN, NOT DAILY OR WEEKLY
content:
Keep the monthly return as the focus. Now you're going to be trading starting with commodities, and you're
focusing on the dollar amount: how much can I make today? How much money can I make this week? And
as soon as you take that first loss for the month, this is when you go into a tailspin because you're in a
hurry to get that back. You don't like taking losses. And I want you to understand something, 27 years from
now you're going to be able to look back and count up an enormous amount of money that if you
collectively add it all up, all of your losses are enormous. And there is nothing wrong with that because
every trader loses, but you got to keep the focus on the monthly return. It doesn't make a difference if you
have three losing trading days out of the week, or you had no winning positive days for the week, but you
come out ahead on the monthly. If your monthly return is consistently higher than one percent, you're doing
exceptionally well. It doesn't feel like it, it doesn't sound like it. You think that everyone that's super elite is
doing 500 percent per month because that's the impression that these books that you're buying and
wasting money on, Michael, are giving you. They don't do that. They aren't making those types of returns,
and you want to be able to focus primarily on what you've done over the course of four weeks or so. And
that result and that return is your goal. You're not trying to get an everyday percentage. You're not trying to
get an every week percentage. You're looking at how you close each month, and is this month positive?
Because if it's positive, that's better than a loss. If you continuously do that, you're going in the right
direction. But try not to rush profits and try not to look beyond the scope of what your monthly return is.

section_title: INVITE THE POSSIBILITY OF BEING WRONG
content:
Invite the possibility of being wrong. I understand obsessive compulsive disorder. We struggle with it, and
we still struggle with it in our forties. But I want you to understand something, Michael, you are not going to
be right 100 percent of the time. And early on, the first couple years, you're spending time trying to perfect
and eliminate the possibility of you being wrong, and it's a farce, don't do that. You're going to waste time
doing that. There is no perfect person, there is no perfect trader. And you still with almost three decades of
experience, I'm speaking to you now, you are still not perfect. Your exits aren't perfect, your entries aren't
perfect all the time. Perfection is still going to evade you, but you do not need perfection. When you're
wrong many times, that's going to lead to a more profitable trade or identifying that you're wrong and
getting out and staying on the sidelines preserves your capital.

section_title: IDENTIFYING FAILURE SIGNATURES AND PRESERVING MENTAL CAPITAL
content:
Invite the possibility of being wrong. Identify the characteristics that leads to your trade idea being wrong.
Identify it before you even put the trade on. So in other words, what negates the idea? What makes it most
likely to fail? If those trades show signatures of that occurring, do not hesitate, just simply close the trade.
Preserve capital. If you do that, you will be on a fast track to preserving your mental capacity, your
willingness to stay studying even though you're not taking trades. If you don't do this, you're going to find
yourself going into the longest tail spin in terms of pursuing something that is eluding you because you're
causing it. You're trying to be perfect and it won't exist. It won't, it won't happen. You're not going to be
there, that will always evade you.

section_title: AVOID CHASING PARABOLIC EQUITY SURGES
content:
Avoid chasing parabolic equity surges. I can recall many of the things that we did and there's some of those
instances where it worked out in our favor, but most of the time it didn't. So avoiding parabolic equity surges
is one of the things that it's going to be very hard for you because the first one you get with Chicago Board
Trade wheat that sets the stage for you thinking that you can do this every week and it doesn't work that
way. Avoid trying to allow your account to grow exponentially with these parabolic equity surges where
you're getting 100 percent plus returns quickly. Try not to do that because number one, you have to have
the right market conditions, you have to have everything on point: your entry, your stop, your trade
management, your exits. There are so many things that have to line up for you to deliver with Olympic level
equity surges on a consistent basis, it's not realistic going into this now. It's going to eventually get to the
point where you can see these things and predict them, but it won't be in the first few years. You're going to
make the mistake in believing that you're better than you really are far before you really get it on what it is
you should be doing as a trader.

section_title: STRATEGIC LEVERAGE REDUCTION AFTER LOSSES
content:
Reduce leverage when taking losses. Now this is something that I can remember we did very poorly. When
we would take losses, you would increase the amount of contracts trying to recapture and get more equity
back in the account because we didn't like the way it felt when money was taken out. Not only did we not
like the fact that we were wrong, but we didn't like the fact that we were wrong and we were robbed of
equity. And we personified that and made it someone or something that we're going to get revenge on. And
that I can look back and say that that was 90 percent of the biggest losses and the biggest and longest
streaks of losing trades were linked to that very thing. If we would have just simply reduced the leverage,
for instance, if we were trading 10 contracts of the Standard & Poor's futures contract and we took a loss of
two, the next trade should be five lots. And if we take a loss there, next trade should be two lots. And keep
reducing until you make 50 percent of the previous loss back, then you can go back up one scale in the
money management and risk trade management.

section_title: AVOIDING ACCOUNT DRAWDOWN CATASTROPHES
content:
But if you don't do this, you're going to have a wild roller coaster equity swings and many accounts being
dusted if you don't do this. You're not going to build in plateaus in your equity. There's going to be a series
of winning trades and then what's going to eventually come that you don't like to admit, it's going to be a
losing trade or two or three. And if you keep risking the same amount, your equity drawdown is going to be
notable and horrendous if you don't control yourself. So you want to reduce the leverage after taking losses
because then the inevitable is going to happen, you're going to have a series of losses. But if you keep
risking two percent, two percent, two percent, two percent, it's going to have greater impact on the
drawdown that you suffer, and it's much more work on your part to recapture that.

section_title: PATIENCE WITH DRAWDOWN RECOVERY
content:
Be patient with drawdown recovery. Losing still sucks when you get stopped out, it's not fun, but it's a
business transaction. Eventually, around the late 20s, you're going to see it as just that. It still won't feel
good and you'll hate that you have them, but you'll come to grips with it a little bit. But be patient with
drawdown recovery, in other words, don't rush to get it back. If you have a day trade or two that shows a net
loss for the day, stop. The next trading day, it's very easy to start building that back up and don't try to get it
back in one trade. You have made so many errors, I can tell you right now at least three dozen of them and
they were all in soybeans. Those trades ended up being parlayed into huge losses and two lost accounts.
You killed two accounts. You don't see it yet, oh but it's coming. You will have blown two futures accounts
trading the soybean market because you're trying to get back your losses and you get caught in a couple
limit moves and you can't get out of those and you end up suffering and blowing out the account because
you are not being patient.

section_title: AVOIDING THE TRAP OF IMMEDIATE RECOVERY AND REVENGE TRADING
content:
You're not going to be patient and you suffer the drawdown, and the recovery of it is not only timeline basis.
In other words, there's no deadline that you have to meet to get that money back. Immediate is what you
feel like it has to be, but that's the trap. That's the trap you have to avoid that, Michael. If you don't listen to
me, you're going to find yourself chasing loss after loss, after loss, and you're not going to be thinking
clearly. You're going to get even more insanely enraged, okay, it's what it's going to become because you
think that the broker's on the other side clicking buttons just to get you, and I can tell you honestly, looking
back, you were just doing it wrong, and doing it wrong a lot and very frequently. Pursuing something that is
very easily accepted: take the loss, go home for the day, and see what happens tomorrow. If you don't do
that, you're going to dust multiple accounts.

section_title: AVOID LARGE RISK FOR LARGE WINS
content:
Avoid large risk for large wins. Now when you first start trading, you're going to read a couple things in
commodity magazine and it's going to inspire you to trade with a little bit larger risk. And you're going to pull
out your calculator and you're going to be sitting at lunch while at work, and you're going to think, if I take
my account and I risk 8, 10, 12 percent per trade, I can do this or that in the time of the end of the month. If
I do that 12 months in a row, I'll be able to buy this or that. Don't do that. Avoid large risk. You're not going to
be consistent in the beginning, you think you are because the books that you bought are telling you that it's
just simply just do this, do that. And when the indicator says this, that's when you buy, and when indicator
says this, you sell, and you're going to find out very quickly that it doesn't work that way. It's, it's not that
easy.

section_title: EXPECT TO MAKE VELOCITY YOUR EDGE
content:
Expect to make velocity your edge. After spending almost 28 years doing this, I can tell you everyone still
thinks that having a large R multiple, okay, or risk the reward model is the secret. And that's not the secret,
the secret is velocity. Doing something over and over and over again that has an expected result that is
high in probability and flipping the equity, that's velocity. You will in your mid-30s discover how you can take
the same account balance, and while other traders and other methodology may be focusing on trying to get
ten to one or more, you can do exceptionally well doing one to one, two to one because you can use the
lower time frames that you don't appreciate right now, but they are the same market with dozens of setups
per day. But you're not in the position when you first start to start doing those types of trades.

section_title: LEVERAGING VELOCITY WITH LOWER TIMEFRAMES
content:
It's going to be a little bit of time before you understand that, but velocity and turning over the equity without
risking a lot, that beats every race. It beats every trading contest. It beats everything, period, because
you're using the rate of turnover in the equity because you're taking more frequency and you're looking at a
result that is measurable. It's a repeating, recurring pattern that forms every single day, multiple times
intraday. So it's not that you want to take a larger risk to make bigger money, you want to take surgical
strikes and parlay the account up using velocity. It's just a higher frequency trade of a same reoccurring
pattern on a lower time frame. And while other traders are waiting for their 10 to 1, 20 to 1, 30 to 1, that
money can only make whatever the maximum R multiple is going to be for it. But within those long or higher
time frame price swings, you're going to be in there moving back and forth both directions, most of the
money being allocated to the side of the marketplace it's going to move in and still trading counter trend
intraday with less than the risk that you're using when you're trading in sync with the market. And you will
destroy the 10 to 1, 20 to 1, 30 to 1 R multiple type trading models, it's simple math. But you're going to be
distracted, Michael, by all these books and all these things. They're going to draw your attention away from
the simplest of things: is it going to go up? Is it going to go down? How far is going to go up? How far is it
going to go down? At what point is it incorrect and I want to be out of the trade? Now that same criteria just
applied to a one minute or five minute chart, you can take that same equity and flip it over and over and
over and over again within the span of say a weekly range. It takes five candles to form. You may have 30
to 40 trades for the course of the week and they're all one minute setups, but if each one of those pay out
two percent minimum, nothing's going to keep up with that.

section_title: CONSIDER THE NEGATIVES OF FORCING SETUPS
content:
Consider the negatives of forcing setups. Your impatience, Michael, in the beginning, because the first
trade you put on you lose 50 percent of your equity and you're trading options and you didn't fully
understand what you were doing, and option trading is not just simply being right on the marketplace, you
have to beat the time decay as well. You'll learn about that later on with some pain. But consider the
negatives of forcing the setups because you're going to try to force it. You're going to have a level that you
want to be buying at, but you are impatient, and you're going to try to buy it ahead of time. And that is a
problem because you aren't very versed in proper stop placement in the beginning, Michael.

section_title: THE DANGERS OF PREMATURE ENTRY AND IGNORING RULES
content:
You're going to have your stop too tight because you don't want to risk a lot of money, but you are risking
five percent on the trade, and that's too much. You're risking way too much, but you're forcing the entry
sooner than it should be taken, and it is done at a time where you're using the maximum amount of
leverage, which strangles the trade because you have to have a very short, tight stop in a trade that you
entered too soon before the level you want to buy it. So when you're doing that, you're convincing yourself
that you're going to be right and ignoring all of the things that you're doing wrong that is inviting failure. You
have to consider the negatives of it: what is the impact of that? Number one, you're teaching yourself that
not following the rules is okay, that trading ahead of the level before it's made a touch or a retest to it, that
that's normal, that's something you should be doing when it doesn't fit within the model. If you've identified
a level and you want to buy at that level, why are you buying before the level's hit? Because you're forcing
the trade, you're impatient, you want to get in because you're afraid that you're going to miss the move. It
might not hit your level. Well guess what? So what? There's going to be tens of thousands of trades in your
career and you're not going to miss the big money maker because of not getting in early. Getting in early is
always, always, I can tell you now looking back, has always been an error. Always, it never works out in our
favor, so don't do it.

section_title: THE PSYCHOLOGICAL AND RISK BENEFITS OF TAKING PARTIALS
content:
Taking partials is the best thing you will ever do. And I'll say it again, taking partials is the best thing that
you're ever going to do, Michael. The biggest hurdle in your development is being comfortable with holding
the trade. You're going to struggle with that. It's going to cause anxiety. You're going to have heart
palpitations. You're going to have shortness of breath. You're going to feel like you're going to faint a couple
times. You're going to vomit a couple times because some of the trades that you're in in the S&P are huge,
and this the smallest little tick is making you go nuts. Wait around for it, it happens. But taking partials off
while the trade is developing, number one, it feels good to take something off. It feels good and it feels like
you're doing something because even when you're in a trade that's winning, you're going to find that it is
like holding on to a motorcycle at 200 plus miles an hour. It's an incredible rush. But you also want to ride
the end sometimes quickly, and when you get into trade and it's moving in your favor, you want to get there
quick. You want to get there and get you out of the trade and profit because you don't like the discomfort of
not knowing if it's going to hit your target. So the way you're going to best deal with that and have the ability
to hold on to the trade longer into fruition or to get stopped out, because that's part of the plan too, you
have to take something off. So if the trade moves 10 points in your favor, take 10 points off with something,
even if it's one contract or one lot. Take something off, it feels good, it's that cookie, that reward. When it
moves a little bit more, take something off again. And you're going to find by doing that, it helps alleviate
the pain and uncertainty of holding on to that trade until it gets to your profit objective.
PARTIALS:

section_title: FUND THE TRADER, REDUCE EXPOSURE
content:
Taking partials reduces risk while paying. You're going to worry in the beginning about getting stopped out
all the time, and you're going to do a lot of acrobatics with your stop-loss. You're going to move it back
when you think it's dropping against you or raise it up when it's rising back up towards your stop, and you're
going to drive yourself crazy doing that. You have to commit to whatever your stop is going to be based on
sound money management and risk. And once the trade starts moving in your favor, take partials off. By
doing that, not only does it fund you as the trader, but it also reduces the risk because every time you take
something off, you're reducing the exposure that your account and your and your mental capital will
withstand. So you have to constantly manage not only the trade, but you have to manage the trader and its
expectation. Your expectation is you put the trade on because you want to be profitable, so what's the best
thing that you can do as a trader when the trade's in profit? Take something off. Don't be fearful and don't
allow greed to say, oh I have to hold on for everything, it's all or nothing because I'm going to tell you,
Michael, when you do that the first couple years, you're going to be in some trades, for instance coffee.
You're going to be holding onto a coffee trade that's going to move a lot, it's going to move a lot and 35,000
turns into an 8,000 loss because you wanted to get your full target. You have to listen, you have to listen to
this. If you don't do these things, Michael, you're going to regret it. Taking partials is the best thing you're
ever going to do. And taking partials reduces risk while paying you.

section_title: IGNORE UNPROFITABLE TRADERS' ADVICE ON RISK
content:
Ignore unprofitable traders' advice on risk. If someone that you read, if someone that you listen to says
anything against what's being said to you right here, I would put a million dollars on them not being a
profitable trader. Chances are they have no idea what it is to be consistent. They probably had hit and miss
type results. But consistent long-term trading results come by way of funding the positions and funding the
trader or as we say it today, pay the trader. If you don't do those things, there's nothing guaranteeing,
Michael, that your trade's going to hit your target. There's absolutely nothing guaranteeing that, and the
volatility in today's world, which at the time of this recording, we've seen some pretty dramatic volatility. And
your trades, every time you put them on, there's no guarantee that you're going to hit your objective. So you
have to get to the point where you trust yourself and you don't rely on other people's advice about risk
because their advice about your risk is free to them, there's no exposure, and the advice they're giving you
probably isn't sound. So stick to what I'm telling you here, commit to it and own it. Your losses are your
losses, your wins are your wins. No one else can take credit for it, and you shouldn't try to share credit.

section_title: EXPECT THE PAIN
content:
Weather series of consecutive losses. Taking losses is normal, Michael, it doesn't feel good. It sucks, but
unfortunately there's simply no way around it. Every trader loses money. Every trader suffers drawdown.
Professional traders manage how much and they understand they don't know how long it's going to take
before it turns the equity around in order to recovery. You will lose fortunes. Expect the pain. The faster you
can accept this, Michael, and warm up to the idea that imperfection is normal, it's absolutely normal. And
there's going to be books that you're going to buy, it's going to sound like it's the best book in the world.
You're going to buy courses and it's going to sound like you're going to get riches untold and is going to fall
into your account. All of that stuff is nonsense. You're going to lose money and you're going to have losing
streaks. And the amount of money, if we could total all up and look back, it's enormous. But it didn't kill you,
it didn't take you out of the game. It did not defeat you. It beats you up a couple times pretty bad, and your
ego is bruised for, well truth be told, I still feel a little pain from it. But you're going to have to accept the fact
that losing streaks do occur. Now it's been a long time since I had 10 losses in a row, but you're going to
have those and there's nothing wrong with it because it helps you, number one, respect risk because no
one is exempt from being wrong. I still get it wrong, and you're going to get it wrong a lot in the beginning,
prepare for that.

section_title: MULTIPLE LOSSES DO NOT PROVE THE METHOD IS BROKEN
content:
Multiple losses does not prove it is broken. Your trading method is not decided in terms of effectiveness if
you have a series of losing trades. A series of losing trades is something that every trading methodology
will eventually, will eventually plague every methodology. There's, there is not one single trader that has not
endured this. It's a normal thing, but when you experience it and you have to endure it, it feels like it's only
you, right, Michael? But it's not just you. Truth is, everyone has it, but the problem is the people online and
the people you read about in their books are not really honest with you. They're going to tell you nothing
about their losses. They're not going to say anything about losing money. They're only going to focus on the
sweet, sugary coded chapters of their books that says this is what I got rich with, and you can too.

section_title: THE SIX-YEAR JOURNEY
content:
Stick to the method and risk reduction. When you have your first string of losses, Michael, you're going to
want to skip around and try something new, and that's the reason why you bought all those books because
you were looking for the holy grail. And the problem with that is, is every time you went to a new system it
starts off with modest wins, and you fall in love with it and you think you figured it out. And then it goes into
a tailspin and you do things the same way that I've outlined in our discussion here, and you lose track of
what you're doing and lose control of yourself and you enter into a series of losing trades. Your first impulse
is to abandon that method and go to something else. And the problem with that is, is you do this dozens of
times. Now if I could grab you by the lapels and shake you again, it's simply this: stop bouncing around.
Focus with one thing in mind: work with a higher time frame bias, work off of one key level, work off with
one market, and stay on one side of the marketplace until proven obviously that you're on the wrong side. If
you manage your risk, don't force the trades, and you're patient, that alone can be profitable, but you have
to stick with the method. And if you don't reduce the risk when you take losses, you won't have the ability to
stay with the method. The method eventually, it takes six years for you to figure it out by the way. Six years
you figure out exactly who you are as a trader. And it feels like a lifetime. It feels like you're never going to
get it, Michael, I know, I know how it feels, and I wish I could go back and just tell you to your face, suck it
up, just take it because everything changes in your sixth year. Everything changes. It becomes easier. It
becomes far less stressful to put the trade on. You know exactly what you're looking for, you know where
your stop should be. You had to manage to stop, you're not over leveraging your account, you're not forcing
trades, you grow into yourself in the sixth year. But prior to that sixth year, you're like a wild cowboy and just
gunslinging, and you're not fearful of anything. You take on risk that's much larger than you should, you
trade more times than you should. And when you have severe draw down it doesn't bother you. And that's a
problem. You weren't afraid to do anything, and you weren't afraid to get into the marketplace with huge
leverage where one trade could wipe out the account. That's nonsense, and you don't need to do that to do
well. But you have to stick to a method that's very consistent that will have losses and you manage your
risk.

section_title: MACRO TO MICRO AND ADMITTING ERROR
content:
You will be correct more than you're incorrect. It doesn't feel like it now, Michael, but you will be. And the
only way that happens is sticking to the method, sticking to the method, working from the macro down to
the micro, and being willing to admit that you're wrong, and either taking the loss and going home for the
day or changing direction. And in the beginning, it's best if you just take my advice right now, Michael, and
just get out of the marketplace and try not to do the reversal because we lose a lot of money doing that too.
DON'

section_title: OWN YOUR UNIQUE TRADING JOURNEY
content:
Don't look for others to measure against. You're going to look at other people, other traders, other authors,
and you're going to try to say that you want to be like them, and everyone else should not be a measuring
stick to you because to have that stunt your growth. You're saying that that person's accomplishments and
accolades, you want them. Why? Focus on the ones that you're going to do yourself, the ones that you are
going to achieve, those successes and those failures because they're stories that are going to be
significant and meaningful to you, and should anyone sit down with you that has any interest in the
marketplace, they're going to be very interested to hear what you have to say. You have so many
adventures ahead of you, Michael, but so much of the pain that we endure was 120 percent avoidable. So
hopefully you've taken my advice here and are going to work towards trying to eliminate some of the pain
that we endure. I can tell you as an older man, I'd greatly appreciate it if you would. And those of you that
were listening, I hope this was also very beneficial and profitable for your learning and development. Until
next time, I wish you good luck and good trading.
